docs.title: Stack in Java Collections Framework
docs.text: This directory covers Stack implementation in Java, designed for LIFO processing.

docs.title: 1. Stack Core (LIFO processing)
docs.text:
• Stack Core Guide: LIFO, creation, and standard push/pop methods.
  • Stack Internal Workings: Vector resizable array details and synchronization monitor locks.
  • Stack Interview Questions: Tech interview questions, call stacks, and recursive frames.

docs.title: 2. Stack Mini Projects
docs.text:
• 01_Balanced_Parentheses_Checker: Verify balanced bracket syntax.
  • 02_Undo_Redo_System: Simulating edits undo/redo states.
  • 03_Browser_Back_History: Back and forward browser navigation.

Back to Collection Framework Home: JCF Index

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
In programming, we frequently encounter tasks that require processing elements in reverse order of their arrival—such as reversing a string, performing an undo operation in a text editor, or navigating back through browser history.

The Java Collection Framework (JCF) provides the Stack class to represent a collection designed for Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) operations.

docs.title: What is a Stack?
docs.text: A Stack is a linear data structure that models a real-world stack of physical items (like a stack of dinner plates or books). You can only place new elements at the top of the stack and remove elements from the top.

docs.title: Why Do We Need Stack?
docs.text: A Stack enforces strict sequential access rules. It prevents random access to elements in the middle of the collection. This is useful for tracking execution states: * JVM Call Stack: The JVM uses a stack to manage method calls, local variables, and execution frames. * Undo/Redo History: Text editors push user changes onto a stack to support undo actions.

docs.title: The LIFO Principle
docs.text:
A stack operates on the Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) principle:

Push (add) ──> [ Top Node: C ] ──> Pop (remove)
               [ Node B      ]
               [ Bottom Node: A ]

The last element pushed onto the stack is the first one popped off.

docs.title: Basic Initialization
docs.text:
import java.util.Stack;

// Generic format: Stack<Type> stackName = new Stack<>();
Stack<Integer> stack = new Stack<>();

Always use Wrapper classes instead of primitive types.

docs.title: Common Methods
docs.text:
The Stack class provides five dedicated methods:

Method Description Time Complexity push(E item) Pushes the element onto the top of the stack. O(1) pop() Removes and returns the element at the top of the stack. O(1) peek() Returns the element at the top without removing it. O(1) isEmpty() Returns true if the stack contains 0 elements. O(1) search(Object o) Returns the 1-based distance of the element from the top. Returns -1 if not found. O(N)

docs.title: Example: Basic Stack Operations
docs.text:
import java.util.Stack;

public class StackDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Stack<String> libraryStack = new Stack<>();

        // 1. Push elements
        libraryStack.push("Book A");
        libraryStack.push("Book B");
        libraryStack.push("Book C");

        System.out.println("Stack: " + libraryStack); // [Book A, Book B, Book C]

        // 2. Peek top
        System.out.println("Top Book: " + libraryStack.peek()); // Book C

        // 3. Search element (1-based index from the top)
        System.out.println("Distance of Book A from top: " + libraryStack.search("Book A")); // 3
        System.out.println("Distance of Book C from top: " + libraryStack.search("Book C")); // 1

        // 4. Pop elements
        System.out.println("Popped: " + libraryStack.pop()); // Book C
        System.out.println("Popped: " + libraryStack.pop()); // Book B

        System.out.println("Remaining Stack: " + libraryStack); // [Book A]
    }
}

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Prefer Deque for Stacks: Although java.util.Stack is a standard class, it extends the legacy Vector class, meaning all its operations are synchronized. This adds unnecessary overhead. The official Java documentation recommends using ArrayDeque as a Stack instead: 
    import java.util.ArrayDeque;
import java.util.Deque;

Deque<String> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();
  • Check for Empty Stack: Always call isEmpty() before calling pop() or peek() to avoid throwing an EmptyStackException.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Random Element Modification: Avoid calling add(index, element) or get(index) on a Stack. While these inherited methods are technically available because Stack extends Vector, using them violates the stack data structure design.
  • StackOverflowError: In recursive algorithms, failing to define a base case will cause the JVM execution stack to overflow, throwing a StackOverflowError.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Stack is a LIFO data structure.
  • Core methods are push(), pop(), and peek().
  • Java recommends using ArrayDeque for stack implementations in modern code to avoid the synchronization overhead of Vector.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text:
The stack data structure is fundamental to computer science, from local variable memory allocation to parsing expressions. Understanding how to use the LIFO principle is essential for writing algorithmic solutions.

Back to Stacks Home: Stacks Index

docs.title: The Backing Vector Array
docs.text:
In Java, the Stack class extends Vector. It stores elements internally in a resizable object array:

// Inherited fields from Vector:
protected Object[] elementData;
protected int elementCount;

docs.title: 1. Push Operation (push())
docs.text:
When you push an element: 1. The JVM checks if the array capacity needs to grow. 2. The element is appended at the current elementCount index: 

elementData[elementCount] = item;
elementCount++;


Push operation index layout:
Index:   [0]   [1]   [2]   [3]   [4]
Data:   [ A ] [ B ] [   ] [   ] [   ]
                     ^
                elementCount (Next insertion index)

docs.title: 2. Pop Operation (pop())
docs.text:
When you pop an element: 1. It retrieves the element at the top index: elementCount - 1. 2. It removes the reference at that index to allow garbage collection. 3. It decrements the elementCount pointer.

Pop operation index layout:
Index:   [0]   [1]   [2]   [3]
Data:   [ A ] [ B ] [ C ] [   ]
                     ^
                elementCount - 1 (Top of the stack)

docs.title: 3. Method Synchronization
docs.text:
Because Stack extends Vector, almost every method is synchronized using the synchronized keyword:

public synchronized E peek() { ... }
public synchronized E pop() { ... }

This ensures thread safety but degrades performance because only one thread can access the stack at a time.

docs.title: Time Complexities
docs.text:
• push(): O(1) amortized (constant time, unless resizing occurs).
  • pop(): O(1) constant time.
  • peek(): O(1) constant time.
  • search(): O(N) linear time (requires scanning the array).

Back to Stacks Home: Stacks Index

docs.title: Q1: What is the LIFO principle?
docs.text: Answer: LIFO stands for Last-In, First-Out. The last element added to the stack is the first one to be removed.

docs.title: Q2: What is the difference between pop() and peek() methods?
docs.text: Answer: * pop() returns and removes the element at the top of the stack. * peek() returns the element at the top of the stack without removing it.

docs.title: Q3: Why is java.util.Stack considered legacy, and what is its recommended alternative?
docs.text: Answer: Stack extends Vector, which synchronizes every method call. This introduces unnecessary locking overhead in single-threaded applications. The recommended alternative is ArrayDeque, which implements the Deque interface and is unsynchronized.

docs.title: Q4: What does the search() method return?
docs.text: Answer: The search(Object o) method returns the 1-based distance from the top of the stack if the element is found. If the element is not found, it returns -1.

docs.title: Q5: Explain how the Call Stack handles recursive methods.
docs.text:
Answer: When a method is called, the JVM pushes an activation record (or stack frame) containing local variables and parameters onto the Call Stack. If the method calls itself recursively, a new stack frame is pushed for each nested call. When a frame exits, it is popped off the stack, returning execution to the previous frame. If there is no base case, the stack continues to grow until it throws a StackOverflowError.

Back to Stacks Home: Stacks Index

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This mini-project demonstrates how a LIFO stack is used to verify that bracket character pairs ((), [], {}) are correctly balanced.

docs.title: Simulation Flow
docs.text:
1. The system scans the string expression character-by-character.
  2. When it encounters an opening bracket, it pushes it onto the stack.
  3. When it encounters a closing bracket, it pops the top element from the stack and checks if they match.
  4. If they mismatch or the stack is empty, the expression is unbalanced.
  5. If the stack is empty at the end of the scan, the expression is balanced.

docs.title: Class Used
docs.text:
• ArrayDeque: Serves as the Stack backing implementation.

Back to Stacks Home: Stacks Index

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This mini-project demonstrates how two stacks are used to implement an undo/redo system in a text editor or similar application.

docs.title: Simulation Flow
docs.text:
1. Write Action: Pushes the current document state onto the undoStack and clears the redoStack.
  2. Undo Action: Pops the previous state from the undoStack, pushes the current state onto the redoStack, and updates the current document text to the popped state.
  3. Redo Action: Pops the state from the redoStack, pushes the current state onto the undoStack, and updates the current document text to the popped state.

docs.title: Class Used
docs.text:
• ArrayDeque: Serves as the stack backing implementation.

Back to Stacks Home: Stacks Index

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This mini-project simulates browser history navigation (back and forward) using two stacks.

docs.title: Simulation Flow
docs.text:
1. Visit Page: Pushes the current page onto the backStack and clears the forwardStack.
  2. Go Back: Pops the previous page from the backStack, pushes the current page onto the forwardStack, and updates the current page to the popped page.
  3. Go Forward: Pops the page from the forwardStack, pushes the current page onto the backStack, and updates the current page to the popped page.

docs.title: Class Used
docs.text:
• ArrayDeque: Serves as the stack backing implementation.

Back to Stacks Home: Stacks Index

docs.title: Generics in Java Collections Framework
docs.text: This directory covers Generics in Java, which provides compile-time type safety for storing and managing collections elements.

docs.title: 1. Generics Guide (Type Safety)
docs.text:
• Generics Guide: Parameterized classes, generic methods, bounded types, and wildcard parameters.
  • Generics Internal Workings: Compiler checks, type erasure bytecode updates, and bridge methods.
  • Generics Interview Questions: Tech interview questions on PECS rule, raw types, and list wildcards.

Back to Collection Framework Home: JCF Index

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
In Java, we often write classes, interfaces, and methods that perform operations on different types of objects. For example, a list can store integers, strings, or custom employee objects.

Before Java 5, collections stored everything as Object references, requiring programmers to manually cast elements back to their original type. This was error-prone and often caused runtime errors.

Generics (introduced in Java 5) add a way to specify the type of objects a collection can hold as a compile-time parameter.

docs.title: Why Generics? (Problems Without Generics)
docs.text:
Without Generics, code is not type-safe. Consider this example:

import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;

public class WithoutGenerics {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        List names = new ArrayList(); // Raw Type
        names.add("Alice");
        names.add("Bob");
        names.add(100); // No compile error! We mistakenly added an Integer

        // Manual casting is required:
        for (Object name : names) {
            String str = (String) name; // Throws ClassCastException at runtime for 100!
            System.out.println(str);
        }
    }
}

docs.title: The Generics Solution:
docs.text:
With Generics, type checks happen at compile time, eliminating runtime casting errors:

List<String> names = new ArrayList<>();
names.add("Alice");
// names.add(100); // Compile-time error! Prevents bad insertions.

for (String name : names) {
    System.out.println(name); // No manual casting required!
}

docs.title: Creating Generic Classes
docs.text:
A generic class is declared with a type parameter enclosed in angle brackets (<>):

public class Box<T> {
    private T value;

    public void set(T value) {
        this.value = value;
    }

    public T get() {
        return value;
    }
}

docs.title: Instantiating:
docs.text:
Box<Integer> integerBox = new Box<>();
integerBox.set(123);

Box<String> stringBox = new Box<>();
stringBox.set("Hello");

Commonly used type parameter naming conventions: * T - Type * E - Element (used extensively in Collections) * K - Key * V - Value

docs.title: Generic Methods
docs.text:
You can make a single method generic, even if the class is not:

public class ArrayPrinter {
    public static <E> void printArray(E[] array) {
        for (E element : array) {
            System.out.print(element + " ");
        }
        System.out.println();
    }
}

The type parameter <E> is placed before the return type.

docs.title: Generic Interfaces
docs.text:
Interfaces can also define type parameters:

public interface KeyValuePair<K, V> {
    K getKey();
    V getValue();
}

docs.title: Bounded Types
docs.text:
You can restrict the types that can be passed to a type parameter using Bounded Types:

// T must be Number or a subclass of Number (e.g. Integer, Double)
public class Stats<T extends Number> {
    T[] nums;

    public Stats(T[] nums) {
        this.nums = nums;
    }

    public double getAverage() {
        double sum = 0.0;
        for (T num : nums) {
            sum += num.doubleValue(); // doubleValue() is available because T extends Number
        }
        return sum / nums.length;
    }
}

docs.title: Wildcards in Generics
docs.text: The wildcard operator ? represents an unknown type.

docs.title: 1. Unbounded Wildcard (?)
docs.text:
Used when you want to read elements from a collection without caring about its specific type: 

public static void printList(List<?> list) {
    for (Object elem : list) {
        System.out.println(elem);
    }
}

docs.title: 2. Upper Bounded Wildcard (? extends T)
docs.text:
Restricts the unknown type to be T or a subclass of T (Read-only / Producer): 

public static double sumOfList(List<? extends Number> list) {
    double sum = 0.0;
    for (Number n : list) {
        sum += n.doubleValue();
    }
    return sum;
}

docs.title: 3. Lower Bounded Wildcard (? super T)
docs.text:
Restricts the unknown type to be T or a superclass of T (Write-only / Consumer): 

public static void addNumbers(List<? super Integer> list) {
    list.add(1);
    list.add(2);
}

docs.title: Type Erasure (Basic)
docs.text:
Java implements Generics using Type Erasure.

The compiler uses type parameters to verify type safety at compile time, and then erases them in the compiled bytecode, replacing them with raw Object types (or their bounded limits). This ensures backward compatibility with older Java versions.

docs.title: Example: Custom Generic Repository
docs.text:
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;

class Repository<T> {
    private final List<T> items = new ArrayList<>();

    public void add(T item) {
        items.add(item);
    }

    public List<T> getAll() {
        return items;
    }
}

public class GenericsDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Repository<String> repo = new Repository<>();
        repo.add("First Entry");
        repo.add("Second Entry");

        System.out.println("Repo Items: " + repo.getAll()); // [First Entry, Second Entry]
    }
}

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Avoid Raw Types: Always declare collections with their generic types (e.g. List<String> list instead of List list). Using raw types bypasses compile-time checks.
  • Use PECS Rule: Remember **P**roducer **E**xtends, **C**onsumer **S**uper:
  • Use ? extends T if you only read elements from a collection (Producer).
  • Use ? super T if you only write elements into a collection (Consumer).

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Generics are Not Covariant: In Java, arrays are covariant (String[] is a subclass of Object[]), but generics are invariant: List<String> is not a subclass of List<Object>.
  • Cannot Instantiate Type Parameters: You cannot write new T() inside a generic class because the type parameter T is erased at runtime, so the JVM does not know what constructor to call.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Generics provide compile-time type safety.
  • Bounded types restrict parameters using extends.
  • Wildcards (?) allow flexible collections parameters matching.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text:
Generics are a powerful tool in Java, providing type safety and code reusability. By moving type check errors from runtime to compile time, they help prevent bugs and eliminate the need for manual casting.

docs.title: Type Erasure
docs.text: Java implements Generics at the compiler level. Once the compiler verifies that your code is type-safe, it performs Type Erasure: it removes all generic type parameter information from the class files.

docs.title: 1. Replacing Type Parameters with Bounds
docs.text:
• If a type parameter is unbounded (e.g. <T>), it is replaced with Object.
  • If a type parameter is bounded (e.g. <T extends Number>), it is replaced with the first bound class (Number).
Source Code (T is replaced with Object):
class Box<T> {                          class Box {
    T val;             =======>             Object val;
    T get() { return val; }                 Object get() { return val; }
}                                       }

Bounded Source Code (T is replaced with Number):
class MathBox<T extends Number> {       class MathBox {
    T val;             =======>             Number val;
    T get() { return val; }                 Number get() { return val; }
}                                       }

docs.title: 2. Automatic Cast Insertion
docs.text:
Because the compiled class files contain only raw types (Object or bounded types), the compiler automatically inserts manual type casts in the calling bytecode when you retrieve value references:

// Source Code:
Box<String> box = new Box<>();
box.set("Hello");
String val = box.get();

Is translated by the compiler into:

// Translated Bytecode equivalent:
Box box = new Box();
box.set("Hello");
String val = (String) box.get(); // Cast is added automatically by the compiler

docs.title: 3. Bridge Methods
docs.text:
To preserve polymorphism and inheritance contracts in compiled classes that extend generic interfaces or classes, the compiler generates synthetic Bridge Methods:

// Generic Parent:
class Node<T> {
    public void setData(T data) { ... }
}

// Subclass:
class MyNode extends Node<Integer> {
    @Override
    public void setData(Integer data) { ... }
}

During type erasure, Node.setData(T) becomes Node.setData(Object). To make sure MyNode.setData(Integer) override is resolved properly by the JVM at runtime, the compiler generates a hidden bridge method:

// Generated hidden method inside MyNode.class:
public void setData(Object data) {
    setData((Integer) data); // Delegates to the specific Integer method
}

docs.title: Runtime Representation
docs.text:
At runtime, there is no difference between a ArrayList<String> and an ArrayList<Integer>. Both are represented by the same raw ArrayList class.

docs.title: Q1: What are the main benefits of using Generics in Java?
docs.text: Answer: * Type Safety: The compiler checks type compatibility at compile time. * Elimination of Casts: No manual casting is required when retrieving elements. * Code Reusability: Classes, interfaces, and methods can be written once to operate on different types of data.

docs.title: Q2: What is Type Erasure?
docs.text: Answer: Type Erasure is the process where the Java compiler removes generic type parameter information after checking type safety, replacing them with raw Object references or bounded types in the compiled bytecode.

docs.title: Q3: What is the difference between List<Object> and List<?>?
docs.text: Answer: * List<Object> is a concrete list that can hold any Java object, but you cannot assign a List<String> to it because generics are invariant. * List<?> is a wildcard list of an unknown type. You can assign a List<String> or a List<Integer> to it, but you cannot add elements to it (except null) because the compiler does not know what type the list contains.

docs.title: Q4: Explain the PECS rule.
docs.text: Answer: PECS stands for Producer Extends, Consumer Super: * Producer Extends: If you only read elements from a collection (it acts as a producer of data), use ? extends T. * Consumer Super: If you only write elements into a collection (it acts as a consumer of data), use ? super T.

docs.title: Q5: What are Bridge Methods in Generics?
docs.text:
Answer: A bridge method is a synthetic method generated by the Java compiler during type erasure to preserve polymorphism. If a class overrides a method from a generic parent, the compiler adds a bridge method matching the parent's erased signature (which uses Object) and delegates calls to the subclass's specific method signature.
